  • Code of Criminal Procedure, 1973 — Section 438 — Nature of accusation — Business transaction — The nature of an accusation being rooted in a business transaction is not a sufficient ground, by itself, to grant anticipatory bail. Courts must consider the gravity of the allegations, such as cheating and criminal breach of trust, rather than focusing solely on the commercial context of the dispute [Context: The Supreme Court criticized the High Court for granting bail merely because the case arose from a business transaction, ignoring serious allegations of cheating and criminal breach of trust.] — (Para 8).

Prashant Singh Rajput vs. The State of Madhya Pradesh and Anr.

2021 INSC 645

  • Code of Criminal Procedure, 1973 — Section 438 — Anticipatory bail — Cancellation — The appellate court may cancel anticipatory bail granted by the High Court if the order is perverse, illegal, or unjustified due to non-application of mind to material facts or crucial circumstances. While the court does not conduct a mini-trial at the bail stage, it must evaluate whether there existed prima facie or reasonable grounds to believe the accused committed the crime, considering the nature and gravity of the offence and the role attributed to the applicant [Context: The High Court failed to consider deficiencies in the investigation highlighted by the trial court and JMFC, leading to an unjustified grant of anticipatory bail in a murder case.] — (Paras 24, 25, 30).
  • Code of Criminal Procedure, 1973 — Section 173 — Investigation — Further investigation — When a Magistrate directs further investigation after identifying clear deficiencies in the police report, the investigating officer is obligated to address those specific omissions and accusations. A failure by the High Court to notice that the supplementary challan ignored the role of the accused while addressing only others reflects a non-application of mind [Context: The High Court overlooked the JMFC's orders identifying that the investigation was incomplete regarding the role of the accused persons seeking bail.] — (Para 28).

Vipan Kumar Dhir vs. State of Punjab and another

2021 INSC 598

  • Code of Criminal Procedure, 1973 — Section 439 / Cancellation of bail — Cancellation of bail — Supervening circumstances — The cancellation of bail requires cogent and overwhelming circumstances. While not exhaustive, such grounds typically include interference with the administration of justice, evasion of justice, or the abuse of the concession of bail, and bail should not be cancelled in a mechanical manner without considering if supervening circumstances make the accused's freedom non-conducive to a fair trial [Context: The Court noted that the accused remained an absconder for two years after being declared a proclaimed offender and that the bail was granted based on irrelevant factors and flawed parity, warranting interference.] — (Paras 9, 10).
  • Code of Criminal Procedure, 1973 — Section 438 / Anticipatory bail — Anticipatory bail — Extraordinary nature — Parity — Anticipatory bail is extraordinary in nature and courts must ensure the possibility of the accused influencing witnesses, fleeing from justice, or obstructing the investigation is not overlooked. Granting parity with a co-accused is unwarranted when the conduct of the accused seeking bail is materially different, particularly where there is a history of evading the process of law [Context: The High Court's grant of anticipatory bail based on parity with a co-accused was held to be unwarranted given the accused's prolonged absence and specific role in the alleged offence.] — (Paras 10, 14).

HIGH COURT OF JUDICATURE FOR RAJASTHAN vs. THE STATE OF RAJASTHAN & ANR.

2021 INSC 578

  • Constitution of India, 1950 — Article 145 — Administrative power of Chief Justice — Allocation of business — The power to fix the roster and allocate judicial business to individual judges of the Court is vested exclusively in the Chief Justice. A single Judge cannot appropriate this administrative jurisdiction by issuing blanket directions regarding the listing of cases [Context: The Court held that the High Court Judge lacked the jurisdiction to direct the Registrar to not list specific categories of bail applications as matters of extreme urgency.] — (Paras 12, 13).
  • Code of Criminal Procedure, 1973 — Section 437 — Access to justice — Bail — A blanket order prohibiting the listing of bail applications effectively restricts access to justice and infringes upon the right to personal liberty. The determination of whether a case falls within the category of extreme urgency must be left to the discretion of the individual Benches rather than being dictated by sweeping administrative directives [Context: The Court found the High Court order to be an improper encroachment on the rights of undertrials and convicts.] — (Para 14).
  • Code of Criminal Procedure, 1973 — Arrest guidelines — Police powers — General directions to police authorities restraining the power of arrest for offences with a maximum sentence of three years are inappropriate where the Code of Criminal Procedure already provides a procedural framework and established judicial methodology exists for such matters [Context: The Court referred to the guidelines in Arnesh Kumar v. State of Bihar to emphasize that existing law sufficiently governs police powers.] — (Paras 16, 17).

M/s Supreme Bhiwandi Wada Manor Infrastructure Pvt. Ltd. vs. The State of Maharashtra & Anr.

2021 INSC 440

  • Code of Criminal Procedure, 1973 — Section 156(3) — Order for investigation — Pre-cognizance — A Magistrate may order an investigation under Section 156(3) before taking cognizance of an offence. At this stage, there is no requirement for the Magistrate to examine the complainant on oath as mandated under Section 200 of the CrPC, which applies only when cognizance is taken [Context: The High Court erred in setting aside the investigation order on the ground that the complainant was not examined on oath, as the Magistrate had ordered investigation under Section 156(3) prior to taking cognizance.] — (Paras 17, 18, 20).
  • Code of Criminal Procedure, 1973 — Section 200 — Examination of complainant — Magisterial cognizance — The requirement to examine the complainant and witnesses on oath under Section 200 is triggered only when the Magistrate formally takes cognizance of an offence. It does not constrain the Magistrate's power to order a pre-cognizance investigation under Section 156(3) — (Paras 17, 18).
  • Code of Criminal Procedure, 1973 — Section 438 / Anticipatory Bail — Grant of bail — Nature of offence —When considering an application for anticipatory bail, the court must be guided by factors such as the nature and gravity of the offences, the role attributed to the applicant, and the likelihood of the accused influencing the investigation or tampering with evidence. An order granting bail that ignores relevant material or suffers from serious infirmities resulting in a miscarriage of justice is liable to be set aside [Context: The Supreme Court set aside the High Court's grant of anticipatory bail, finding it had ignored the serious allegations of fraudulent misappropriation of funds.] — (Paras 22, 23).

PRATHVI RAJ CHAUHAN vs. UNION OF INDIA & ORS.

2020 INSC 147

  • Scheduled Castes and Scheduled Tribes (Prevention of Atrocities) Act, 1989 — Section 18A — Protective discrimination — Legislative competence — Pre-arrest bail — The provisions of Section 18A are not arbitrary or unconstitutional as they were enacted by Parliament to rectify the impact of judicial directions that had previously diluted the efficacy of the Act. The legislature is competent to enact such provisions to ensure the effective implementation of the Act, which is a facet of protective discrimination mandated by the Constitution [Context: The court held that the provisions of Section 18A, enacted to restore the legal position post the decision in Dr. Subhash Kashinath Mahajan, are constitutionally valid as they serve the objective of protective discrimination for the SC/ST communities.] — (Paras 1, 4, 9, 10).
  • Code of Criminal Procedure, 1973 — Section 438 — Anticipatory bail — Prima facie case — Inherent powers — Though Section 18 and 18A of the Act of 1989 prohibit the grant of anticipatory bail, the courts retain the power to grant such relief in exceptional cases where the complaint does not make out a prima facie case for the applicability of the Act. This power must be exercised sparingly to prevent abuse of the process of law without violating the statutory bar [Context: The Court clarified that while Section 18 and 18A bar anticipatory bail, this bar does not apply where no prima facie case is made out.] — (Paras 10, 11, 17, 19, 20).
  • Constitution of India, 1950 — Article 15, Article 17, Article 21 — Fraternity — Equality — Untouchability — The right to live with dignity under Article 21 and the constitutional mandate of fraternity necessitate the enactment and enforcement of laws protecting Scheduled Castes and Scheduled Tribes from atrocities. Protective discrimination is a constitutional imperative to uplift these historically oppressed classes and does not result in reverse discrimination [Context: The court emphasized that the Act is a necessary instrument to achieve the constitutional ideal of fraternity and equality for vulnerable sections.] — (Paras 41, 42, 47, 51, 2, 3, 4, 12).

Sushila Aggarwal and others vs. State (NCT of Delhi) and another

2020 INSC 81

  • Code of Criminal Procedure, 1973 — Section 438 — Anticipatory bail — Duration — Scope — Anticipatory bail granted under Section 438 is not required to be invariably limited to a fixed period or till the filing of the charge sheet. The protection can continue until the conclusion of the trial, depending upon the facts and circumstances of the case [Context: The Court overruled decisions imposing inflexible time-bound restrictions on anticipatory bail.] — (Para 77).
  • Code of Criminal Procedure, 1973 — Section 438 — Blanket order — Conditions — While granting anticipatory bail, the Court is not precluded from imposing conditions under Section 438(2) read with Section 437(3). Blanket orders granting immunity from arrest for all future offences are prohibited, and bail must relate to a specific offence or incident [Context: The Court clarified that while conditions can be imposed, they must be tailored to the facts of the case rather than applied mechanically.] — (Para 78).
  • Code of Criminal Procedure, 1973 — Section 439(2) — Cancellation of bail — Supervening circumstances —The power of the Court to direct an accused to be taken into custody is governed by Section 439(2). This power can be exercised if there is a violation of bail conditions or if supervening circumstances arise that render freedom of the accused inconsistent with a fair trial, irrespective of whether the bail was granted under Section 438 or Section 439 [Context: The Court clarified that the investigating agency can approach the Court under Section 439(2) if an accused on anticipatory bail violates conditions or if the investigation requires custody.] — (Para 78).

SHRI P. CHIDAMBARAM vs. CENTRAL BUREAU OF INVESTIGATION

2019 INSC 1113

  • Code of Criminal Procedure, 1973 — Section 439 — Bail — Economic offences — Flight risk — Influencing witnesses — The factors to be considered for grant of bail include the nature of the accusation, severity of punishment, nature of material relied upon by the prosecution, reasonable apprehension of tampering with witnesses, the possibility of the accused absconding, and the character and standing of the accused. Bail cannot be denied to an individual based on the conduct of other economic offenders or generalized, unsubstantiated apprehensions of witness tampering when the material record lacks evidence to support such claims [Context: The Court granted regular bail to the appellant, noting that the flight risk was mitigated by passport surrender and look-out notices, and the apprehension of influencing witnesses was not supported by any contemporaneous material in the remand applications.] — (Paras 22, 27, 31, 32).
  • Bail — Prima facie findings — Merits of the case — At the stage of considering a bail application, courts must avoid elaborate examination of evidence and detailed documentation of the merits of the case, as this may prejudice the accused at trial. While the court must indicate reasons for its prima facie conclusion, it should not express conclusive findings on merits that could influence subsequent proceedings [Context: The Court clarified that the findings in the High Court judgment on merits should not influence the trial or other proceedings.] — (Paras 17, 18).

UNION OF INDIA vs. STATE OF MAHARASHTRA AND ORS.

2019 INSC 1064

  • Constitution of India, 1950 — Article 142 — Power of Supreme Court — Judicial legislation — Legislative domain — The power conferred on the Court by Article 142 is curative in nature and cannot be used to supplant substantive law or build a new edifice where none existed earlier by ignoring express statutory provisions. Directions issued under this power cannot override specific legislative schemes or operate in conflict with valid statutes, nor can they be used to conduct judicial legislation which amounts to determination of policy [Context: The Court held that the guidelines issued in the earlier judgment regarding the Atrocities Act encroached upon the field reserved for the legislature and were contrary to the protective discrimination mandate.] — (Paras 21, 22, 67).
  • Scheduled Castes and the Scheduled Tribes (Prevention of Atrocities) Act, 1989 — Section 18 — Anticipatory bail — Protective discrimination — The prohibition on anticipatory bail under Section 18 is justified by the prevailing social conditions and the history of untouchability, as victims are vulnerable to intimidation by the dominant class. Such exclusion does not violate Article 14 or Article 21, as these offences constitute a distinct and special class [Context: The Court reaffirmed the validity of Section 18 as previously upheld in Ram Krishna Balothia, noting that misused provisions should be handled through Section 482 CrPC rather than legislative-style guidelines.] — (Paras 18, 19, 54).
  • Code of Criminal Procedure, 1973 — Section 154 — Registration of FIR — Preliminary inquiry — Upon receipt of information disclosing a cognizable offence, a police officer is mandatorily required to register an FIR forthwith. No preliminary inquiry is permissible prior to such registration, and any guidelines requiring such inquiry are contrary to the settled law in Lalita Kumari [Context: The Court recalled the direction mandating a preliminary inquiry for cases under the Atrocities Act, as it placed victims in a disadvantageous position compared to complainants under general law.] — (Paras 16, 17, 65).
  • Constitution of India, 1950 — Article 15(4) / Equality — Protective discrimination — Caste-based discrimination — Legislative and judicial measures for the protection of Scheduled Castes and Scheduled Tribes are mandated by the Constitution to achieve equality. Any procedure that treats members of these classes with doubt or creates barriers for them that are not applicable to the general class is violative of the equality principle under Article 14 [Context: The Court found that the impugned guidelines requiring approvals for arrest were discriminatory against the protected classes.] — (Paras 47, 51, 53).

P. CHIDAMBARAM vs. DIRECTORATE OF ENFORCEMENT

2019 INSC 965

  • Code of Criminal Procedure, 1973 — Section 438 — Anticipatory bail — Exceptional cases — The power to grant anticipatory bail under Section 438 is extraordinary and should be exercised sparingly. It is not an integral part of Article 21 of the Constitution and should be granted only in exceptional cases where the court is convinced that the accusation is frivolous or groundless. The court must strike a delicate balance between safeguarding individual liberty and the larger societal interest [Context: The court refused to grant anticipatory bail to the appellant, noting that the case involved serious allegations of money laundering and that such relief would hamper the effective investigation.] — (Paras 67, 69, 70).
  • Prevention of Money-Laundering Act, 2002 — Section 19 — Power to arrest — Statutory safeguards —Section 19 provides the power of arrest to specified officers on the basis of material in their possession and reasons to believe recorded in writing. The Act includes robust safeguards, including the requirement to record reasons for arrest and the production of the arrested person before a Judicial or Metropolitan Magistrate within twenty-four hours, ensuring that the power of arrest is not exercised arbitrarily [Context: The court held that the procedural safeguards in the Act were sufficient and that granting anticipatory bail would scuttle the statutory power of the investigating officers.] — (Paras 33, 34).
  • Code of Criminal Procedure, 1973 — Section 172 — Case diary — Confidentiality — Judicial perusal — A court has the unfettered power under Section 172(2) to examine case diaries to satisfy its judicial conscience regarding the direction and correctness of an investigation. However, neither the accused nor their agents are entitled to access these diaries, as confidentiality is essential for protecting informants and ensuring the integrity of the investigation. The court's perusal of such materials is an aid to the judicial process and not to be used as evidence against the accused [Context: The court upheld the practice of receiving and perusing materials collected by the prosecution in a sealed cover to satisfy itself of the necessity of custodial interrogation.] — (Paras 49, 51, 52, 53).
  • Criminal Procedure / Investigation — Custodial interrogation — Mini trial — Judicial non-interference —Investigation of a cognizable offence is the exclusive prerogative of the investigating agency, and the judiciary should generally avoid interfering in the course of investigation. The manner, venue, and timing of interrogation are matters for the investigator, and the court cannot hold a mini trial at the anticipatory bail stage to assess whether an accused's responses during interrogation were satisfactory or evasive [Context: The court rejected the request to order the production of transcripts of the appellant's interrogation.] — (Paras 57, 58, 59, 64).
  • Prevention of Money-Laundering Act, 2002 — Economic offences — Class apart — Economic offences constitute a class apart because they are committed with cool calculation, deliberate design, and an eye on personal profit, posing a serious threat to the nation's financial integrity. Consequently, courts must adopt a different approach in bail matters, prioritizing the larger public interest and the effective unearthing of the money trail over the individual's request for pre-arrest protection [Context: The court emphasized that custodial interrogation is a recognized and more effective mode of investigation for economic offences, especially where money laundering involves placement and layering stages.] — (Paras 77, 78, 79, 82).

PRAKASH CHAND MEENA vs. THE STATE OF RAJASTHAN & ANR.

2018 INSC 942

  • Code of Criminal Procedure, 1973 — Section 438 — Anticipatory bail — Protection — The protection granted by this Court under Section 438 Cr.P.C. is an interim measure, and the Trial Court should consider the merits of the case when deciding on regular bail rather than relying solely on the existence of anticipatory bail [Context: The appellant was granted anticipatory bail by the Supreme Court, which the Trial Court later used as the basis to grant regular bail without independent consideration.] — (Paras 2, 3, 4).
  • Bail — Modification of conditions — The de facto complainant is at liberty to approach the Trial Court to seek modification of the conditions of bail granted to an accused [Context: The Court clarified that the de facto complainant could move the Trial Court regarding bail conditions despite the disposal of the appeal.] — (Paras 5, 8).

DWARIKA DAS RATHI vs. THE STATE OF CHHATTISGARH

2018 INSC 941

  • Code of Criminal Procedure, 1973 — Section 438 — Anticipatory bail — Conditions for bail — Interim protection — The Court may grant interim protection from arrest upon the condition of cooperation with the investigation and the deposit of a specified sum related to the alleged financial deficit. If the accused is unable to deposit the full sum, the State may be permitted to attach and sell assets to secure the recovery [Context: The interim protection granted by the Court was made absolute, with liberty to the State to attach the appellant's assets to cover the deficit, while preserving the appellant's right to challenge the quantification of the liability before the Trial Court.] — (Paras 2, 5, 7, 9).

Prem Giri vs. State of Rajasthan

2018 INSC 445

  • Code of Criminal Procedure, 1973 — Section 438 — Anticipatory bail — Reasoned order — Duty of court —A judicial order rejecting an application for anticipatory bail must assign reasons for the dismissal. Failing to provide reasons for the denial of such a vital liberty constitutes a material error that requires the order to be set aside and the matter remanded for fresh consideration [Context: The High Court had dismissed the appellant's anticipatory bail application without providing reasons, despite an earlier remand by the Supreme Court for the same reason in the same case.] — (Paras 7, 8, 13, 14).

SEEMA SINGH vs. CENTRAL BUREAU OF INVESTIGATION & ANR.

2018 INSC 364

  • Code of Criminal Procedure, 1973 — Section 439 — Bail — Discretionary power — The grant or denial of bail is entirely the discretion of the judge hearing the matter. While such discretion is unfettered, it must be exercised judiciously, cautiously, and in compliance with established principles. Courts must avoid elaborate examinations of evidence at the bail stage that might prejudice the accused but should indicate reasons for the prima facie conclusion to grant or refuse bail [Context: The High Court's exercise of discretion to grant bail was found not to be perverse, as it considered relevant factors and provided a reasoned order.] — (Paras 10, 11, 13, 14).
  • Criminal Law — Presumption of innocence — Bail vs Jail — A fundamental postulate of criminal jurisprudence is the presumption of innocence. The grant of bail is the general rule, and incarceration is an exception. Judicial discretion in granting bail must be exercised in a humane and compassionate manner, ensuring conditions are not so strict as to make the grant of bail illusory — (Para 14).
  • Criminal Law — Expert opinion — Evidentiary value — Expert evidence is intended to assist the court in reaching a final conclusion and is not conclusive in itself. Courts must analyze such reports in conjunction with other evidence on record, particularly when the reports are based on studies or photographs rather than a direct postmortem of the body, and their evidentiary value is subject to cross-examination during trial [Context: The reports from CRRI, AIIMS, CFSL, and IIT were considered as material to be tested at trial rather than conclusive proof at the bail stage.] — (Paras 12, 13).

DR. SUBHASH KASHINATH MAHAJAN vs. THE STATE OF MAHARASHTRA AND ANR.

2018 INSC 273

  • Scheduled Castes and the Scheduled Tribes (Prevention of Atrocities) Act, 1989 — Section 18 — Anticipatory bail — Bar on jurisdiction — Prima facie case — The bar on grant of anticipatory bail under Section 18 does not apply where no prima facie case of an offence under the Act is made out or where the allegations are patently mala fide and false. Courts have inherent jurisdiction to examine whether a prima facie case exists before applying the statutory bar to ensure that the Act is not used as an instrument for blackmail or personal vengeance [Context: The Court held that the proceedings against the appellant were an abuse of process as the denial of sanction for prosecution in an official capacity did not constitute an offence under the Act.] — (Paras 51, 60, 63, 74).
  • Constitution of India, 1950 — Article 21 — Personal liberty — Due process — Procedure established by law — Procedures established by law that deprive a person of life or liberty must be fair, just, and reasonable. This Court has the power to issue directions and guidelines to protect citizens against arbitrary arrest and abuse of power by the state or individuals, even where such directions are perceived as legislative, until appropriate legislation is enacted [Context: The Court issued guidelines for arrest under the SC/ST Act to prevent indiscriminate and arbitrary arrests that violate constitutional rights.] — (Paras 31, 32, 36, 41, 76).
  • Code of Criminal Procedure, 1973 — Section 41 and Section 41A — Power of arrest — Preliminary enquiry — Safeguards — Arrest is not a mandatory consequence of the registration of a cognizable offence. Police officers must verify the necessity of arrest based on credible information and recorded reasons to prevent the abuse of power, particularly in cases under special statutes where the potential for misuse is high [Context: The Court directed that arrest under the Atrocities Act, in the absence of other independent offences, requires prior written permission from the appointing authority for public servants or the Senior Superintendent of Police for others.] — (Paras 12, 14, 36, 79, 81).

Prem Giri vs. State of Rajasthan

2017 INSC 1234

  • Code of Criminal Procedure, 1973 — Section 438 — Anticipatory bail — Duty to assign reasons — Orders rejecting applications for anticipatory bail must contain reasons. A mechanical order lacking judicial reasoning, particularly one stating merely that the court does not deem it proper to grant bail after considering the overall facts, reflects non-application of judicial mind [Context: The High Court's order rejecting bail was set aside for failing to assign reasons and the matter was remanded for fresh consideration.] — (Paras 7, 8, 9, 11).
  • Bail — Grant or rejection of bail — Requirement of reasons — While a detailed examination of evidence is not required at the stage of granting bail, courts must indicate prima facie reasons for their decision. Any order devoid of such reasons suffers from non-application of mind — (Para 10).

Barun Chandra Thakur vs. Central Bureau of Investigation and Others.

2017 INSC 1214

  • Code of Criminal Procedure, 1973 — Section 438 — Anticipatory bail — Concurrent jurisdiction — A person is entitled to approach the High Court directly for anticipatory bail under Section 438 because the High Court and the Sessions Court have concurrent jurisdiction. The invocation of such jurisdiction cannot be faulted merely on the basis that the alternative forum was available [Context: The Court upheld the High Court's grant of interim bail to the private respondents despite objections regarding their direct approach to the High Court.] — (Para 9).
  • Criminal Procedure / Interim Bail — Interim protection — Pending investigation — Grant of interim bail pending investigation is justified when the investigating agency is unable to point to any evidence of complicity or involvement of the accused in the crime. Courts should exercise caution when the investigation remains at a preliminary stage based merely on theories of possibility [Context: The Court refused to interfere with the order granting interim bail to the respondents as there was no evidence linking them to the crime.] — (Paras 10, 11, 12).
  • Bar Association — Resolution not to represent — Resolutions passed by Bar Associations refusing to represent an accused are against the ethics of the legal profession and are deprecated by the Court [Context: The Court noted that the resolution had been withdrawn.] — (Para 3).

FEKAN YADAV vs. SATENDRA YADAV @ BOSS YADAV @ SATENDRA KUMAR AND ORS.

2017 INSC 958

  • Code of Criminal Procedure, 1973 — Section 438 / Anticipatory bail — Anticipatory bail — Gravity of accusation — Custodial interrogation — The High Court must assign valid reasons when granting anticipatory bail, particularly in cases involving serious accusations like kidnapping. Where the victim remains untraced and witnesses support the prosecution's case, the grant of anticipatory bail without considering the gravity of the offence is unjustified [Context: The Supreme Court set aside the High Court's order granting anticipatory bail, noting the gravity of the accusations and the lack of reasons provided by the High Court.] — (Para 8).

THE STATE OF TELANGANA vs. HABIB ABDULLAH JEELANI & ORS

  • Code of Criminal Procedure, 1973 — Section 482 / Inherent powers of High Court — Quashment of FIR — Interim protection from arrest — While the High Court possesses wide inherent powers under Section 482 CrPC to quash an FIR to prevent abuse of process or secure the ends of justice, it cannot, while declining to quash the investigation, pass an order restraining the investigating agency from arresting the accused. Such a direction is in the nature of anticipatory bail, which is not maintainable without satisfying the conditions of Section 438 CrPC [Context: The High Court had erroneously directed that the accused not be arrested during the investigation after refusing to stay the investigation itself; this order was set aside.] — (Paras 15, 23, 25).
  • Code of Criminal Procedure, 1973 — Section 154 — Registration of FIR — Preliminary inquiry —Registration of an FIR is mandatory under Section 154 CrPC if the information discloses a cognizable offence. The reasonableness, credibility, or veracity of the information is not a condition precedent for registration, though a limited preliminary inquiry may be conducted in certain specific categories of cases to ascertain the presence of a cognizable offence — (Paras 6, 8, 9).
  • Code of Criminal Procedure, 1973 — Section 438 / Anticipatory bail — Grant of bail — Conditional order —A court cannot issue a blanket order restraining arrest, nor can it direct that an accused be admitted to bail upon surrender in a manner that bypasses the statutory requirements of Section 438 CrPC. Any interim relief must strictly conform to the statutory command of Section 438 and be imposed with suitable conditions — (Paras 16, 24).

RAMESH KUMAR vs. THE STATE OF NCT OF DELHI

  • Code of Criminal Procedure, 1973 — Section 438 — Anticipatory bail — Imposition of conditions — In proceedings for anticipatory bail under Section 438 of the Code, courts should not transform the process into a mechanism for the recovery of money or impose conditions that are onerous, unreasonable, or unrelated to the fairness of the investigation or trial. Conditions for bail must focus on ensuring the accused's appearance, preventing the tampering of evidence, and maintaining the safety of the community [Context: The Supreme Court set aside the High Court's condition requiring the appellant to deposit money as a pre-requisite for anticipatory bail in a private cheating dispute.] — (Paras 2, 22, 26).
  • Indian Penal Code, 1860 — Section 420 — Cheating — Civil dispute — The process of criminal law should not be pressed into service to settle disputes that are predominantly civil in nature. The court must avoid using criminal proceedings for the recovery of alleged dues in such disputes [Context: The Court observed that the dispute between the appellant and the complainants over property redevelopment and payment was essentially civil in nature.] — (Paras 24, 27).
  • Criminal Procedure / Inherent Powers of Quashing — Third-party intervention — Complainants have no automatic right of audience in criminal proceedings concerning bail unless the situation involves the potential compounding of an offence with the court's permission [Context: The Court dismissed the intervention application filed by the complainants.] — (Paras 33, 34).

Ramesh Chandra Vaishya vs. The State of Uttar Pradesh & Anr.

  • Scheduled Castes and the Scheduled Tribes (Prevention of Atrocities) Act, 1989 — Section 3(1)(x) — Place within public view — Caste-related abuses — To attract an offence under Section 3(1)(x), the insult or intimidation must occur in a place within public view, and the utterance must be specifically targeted at the victim because they are a member of a Scheduled Caste or Scheduled Tribe. Merely hurling caste-related abuses without the presence of a third-party member of the public, or without proof that the insults were specifically targeted at the victim's caste, fails to satisfy the essential ingredients of the provision [Context: The court found no evidence of any member of the public present at the time of the incident, nor did the charge-sheet specify the nature of the utterances, thus quashing the charge under the SC/ST Act.] — (Paras 17, 18).
  • Indian Penal Code, 1860 — Section 323 — Voluntarily causing hurt — Investigation — A prosecution for voluntarily causing hurt under Section 323 must be supported by material evidence such as a medical report or corroborating testimony, particularly when the complainant's version is contested. A perfunctory investigation conducted in haste, ignoring the absence of evidence for the nature of hurt and medical reports, warrants interference to prevent abuse of the process of law [Context: The lack of medical evidence and the failure of the prosecution to substantiate the complainant's injuries led the court to conclude that the proceedings under Section 323 should not continue.] — (Para 21).
  • Indian Penal Code, 1860 — Section 504 — Intentional insult — Public peace — Mere abuse of a complainant does not satisfy the requirements of Section 504. The offence is complete only when there is an intentional insult of such a degree that it is intended or known to be likely to provoke the person insulted to break the public peace or commit any other offence [Context: The court held that in the absence of evidence showing the insults were intended to provoke a breach of the public peace, the charge under Section 504 could not be sustained.] — (Paras 22, 23).
  • Code of Criminal Procedure, 1973 — Section 482 — Inherent powers — Quashing of proceedings — The High Court has the power under Section 482 to quash criminal proceedings when it is clear that the allegations, even if accepted at face value, do not disclose the commission of an offence or when the continuation of proceedings would be an abuse of the process of law [Context: The High Court's refusal to quash the proceedings was set aside because the charge-sheet lacked sufficient material to make out a prima facie case.] — (Paras 9, 24, 25).

OMPRAKASH SAHNI vs. JAI SHANKAR CHAUDHARY & ANR. ETC.

  • Code of Criminal Procedure, 1973 — Section 389 — Suspension of sentence — Bail pending appeal — An appellate court exercising powers under Section 389 should not re-appreciate evidence or conduct a mini-trial at the stage of suspension of sentence. The court must consider whether there are exceptional circumstances or palpable defects on the face of the record suggesting the conviction may not be sustainable, rather than merely relying on the absence of misuse of liberty during trial [Context: The High Court erred by re-appreciating evidence, such as delays in FIR registration and overwriting, to grant bail in a murder conviction; the Supreme Court set aside this order.] — (Paras 20, 24, 33, 34).
  • Criminal Law — Presumption of innocence — Post-conviction — Upon conviction by a court of competent jurisdiction, the initial presumption of innocence is erased. While the court may still suspend a sentence and grant bail, the threshold is significantly higher than at the pre-conviction stage, and it should not be done as a matter of routine — (Paras 23, 28, 30).

ANSAR AHMAD vs. STATE OF UTTAR PRADESH AND ANR

  • Criminal Procedure — Section 439 / Bail — Cancellation of bail — Challenge to order granting bail — Distinction between — An application for cancellation of bail usually requires showing supervening circumstances such as misuse of liberty. However, an application challenging the order of grant of bail focuses on the illegality, perversity, or arbitrary exercise of discretion by the court, and such an order can be quashed without necessitating proof of subsequent misconduct [Context: The court distinguished between the two types of applications to affirm its power to set aside the High Court's bail order due to the failure to consider relevant material.] — (Paras 15, 16).
  • Criminal Procedure — Section 439 / Bail — Relevant considerations — Judicial application of mind — An order granting bail must reflect a judicial application of mind regarding established parameters, including the nature of the accusation, severity of punishment, status of the accused, risk of tampering with evidence or influencing witnesses, and the potential for repetition of the offence [Context: The court found that the High Court failed to account for the respondents' criminal antecedents and the ongoing trial risks when granting bail.] — (Paras 10, 11).
  • Criminal Procedure — Section 439 / Bail — Witness tampering — Recording of evidence — It is expedient in the interest of the criminal justice system that bail is considered after ensuring that the statements of vital witnesses have been recorded, particularly when there is a significant risk of influencing evidence [Context: The court noted that crucial eyewitnesses had not yet been examined, justifying the cancellation of bail.] — (Para 12).

Suneetha Narreddy vs. Y S Avinash Reddy & Anr

  • Code of Criminal Procedure, 1973 — Section 438 — Anticipatory bail — Ad interim stay of arrest — Courts exercising power under Section 438 should not pass extraordinary directions that stultify the course of investigation, such as mandating that interrogation of a suspect be conducted solely in a printed or written form or through a questionnaire. Such procedural constraints on investigative agencies during the pendency of bail applications are unwarranted and potentially prejudicial to the discovery of truth [Context: The Supreme Court set aside the High Court's order which had granted ad interim protection from arrest coupled with directives for the CBI to conduct examination of the respondent only through a written questionnaire.] — (Paras 27, 29, 30).

MS. X vs. THE STATE OF MAHARASHTRA AND ANOTHER

  • Code of Criminal Procedure, 1973 — Section 438 — Anticipatory bail — Relevant considerations — While considering an application for anticipatory bail, the court must balance the nature and gravity of the offence, the role of the accused, the likelihood of influencing the investigation or tampering with evidence, and the potential for fleeing justice. The court should refrain from conducting a detailed evidentiary analysis at this stage to avoid prejudicing the trial, but it must record reasons why it is granting bail, particularly in serious offences [Context: The High Court failed to consider these parameters and was unduly swayed by alleged variations in the prosecutrix's statement, leading the Supreme Court to set aside the bail order.] — (Paras 15, 16).
  • Code of Criminal Procedure, 1973 — Section 437(5) and Section 439(2) — Cancellation of bail — Addition of serious offences — When a serious, non-bailable offence is added to the FIR after the grant of bail, the investigating agency or the court is empowered to direct the accused to surrender or be taken into custody. The court granting bail or an appellate court can order the arrest of an accused already on bail upon the introduction of graver charges if the initial bail order is found to be based on an erroneous appreciation of the material on record [Context: The Court held that the High Court failed to recognize that the addition of Section 376 IPC necessitated a fresh assessment of the bail plea.] — (Paras 19, 20, 21).
  • Victim's Rights — Right to be heard — Participation in trial — The victim in a criminal proceeding, particularly in serious offences, has a statutory and legal right to be heard at every crucial stage of the proceedings, including bail hearings. Denying the victim an opportunity to participate or present their stance before the court during bail proceedings amounts to a failure in the administration of criminal justice [Context: The Supreme Court noted that the High Court failed to provide a meaningful hearing to the prosecutrix despite her intervention application.] — (Paras 23, 24, 25).

BIMLA TIWARI vs. STATE OF BIHAR & ORS.

  • Criminal Procedure / Bail — Pre-arrest bail — Money recovery proceedings — The process of criminal law, particularly in matters of grant of bail, is not akin to money recovery proceedings. Criminal law cannot be utilised for arm-twisting or to facilitate money recovery while opposing or granting bail [Context: The Court annulled the High Court's condition of payment as a prerequisite for pre-arrest bail, emphasizing that recovery of money is a civil matter.] — (Paras 9, 10, 11).
  • Code of Criminal Procedure, 1973 — Section 438 — Anticipatory bail — Conditions — Discretion to grant pre-arrest or regular bail must be exercised with reference to the material on record and established parameters governing bail, rather than based on the offering or payment of money. Courts should not ordinarily impose payment conditions in bail orders [Context: The Supreme Court affirmed the grant of pre-arrest bail to the respondents but struck down the High Court's order requiring a payment of Rs. 75,000 to the informant.] — (Paras 10, 11, 16).
  • Code of Criminal Procedure, 1973 — Section 82 — Absconding — Issuance of process — While the petitioner urged that pre-arrest bail should not be granted after the issuance of process under Section 82, the Court in the facts of the case chose not to disturb the grant of bail, focusing instead on the improper imposition of financial conditions [Context: The contention regarding the illegality of granting bail post-issuance of process was noted but ultimately superseded by the Court's ruling on the nature of the proceedings.] — (Paras 6, 7, 8).

SUNITA DEVI & ANR. vs. THE STATE OF HARYANA

  • Code of Criminal Procedure, 1973 — Section 438 / Anticipatory Bail — Custodial interrogation — Cooperation with investigation — When an accused has already joined the investigation and there is no allegation of lack of cooperation, custodial interrogation is unnecessary, and the court may grant anticipatory bail. The court retains the right to cancel bail if the accused fails to cooperate with the investigating agency at any subsequent stage [Context: The court granted anticipatory bail to the appellants, setting aside the High Court's refusal, noting that the appellants had joined the investigation and were accused of cheating in land transactions.] — (Paras 1, 2).

Amy Mehta vs. State of Karnataka & Anr.

  • Code of Criminal Procedure, 1973 — Section 439 — Bail — Relevant considerations — While considering an application for bail under Section 439 of the Code, the court must take into account the seriousness of the offences, the nature of the allegations, and the material collected during investigation as part of the chargesheet. A failure to consider these factors renders an order granting bail unsustainable [Context: The High Court's grant of bail was set aside because it failed to consider the seriousness of the offence and the material in the chargesheet.] — (Paras 2.3, 2.5, 3).
  • Criminal Procedure / Bail — Custodial trial — Relevance in bail — The necessity of custodial interrogation is a relevant factor primarily for anticipatory bail under Section 438 of the Code, not for regular bail under Section 439 after the chargesheet has already been filed [Context: The Court clarified that the High Court's reliance on the lack of need for further custodial trial was an irrelevant factor for a regular bail application.] — (Para 2.4).

MR. VINAY PRAKASH SINGH vs. SAMEER GEHLAUT & ORS.

  • Code of Criminal Procedure, 1973 — Section 428 — Set-off — Period of detention — Custody — For the purpose of set-off under Section 428, the detention undergone must be in connection with the same case. Pre-trial custody in another case cannot be set off against a sentence of imprisonment imposed in a subsequent contempt proceeding simply because the contemnor was produced from jail for the purpose of such proceedings [Context: The Court held that the applicant was not entitled to count the period spent in custody in another case as detention in the contempt case for the purposes of Section 428.] — (Paras 9, 12, 13, 14).
  • Law of Precedents — Euclid's Theorem — Context of law — A judgment of a Court is not to be read as Euclid's Theorem shorn of the facts and the context in which the law has been declared. Legal propositions laid down in a case must be understood in light of the specific jurisdictional questions the Court was addressing at that time [Context: The Court clarified that the definition of custody in Niranjan Singh, being in the context of bail jurisdiction under Section 439 CrPC, did not entitle the applicant to claim set-off under Section 428 in the present matter.] — (Para 11).

SUBRATA ROY SAHARA vs. PRAMOD KUMAR SAINI & ORS.

  • Code of Criminal Procedure, 1973 — Section 438 — Anticipatory bail — Scope of inquiry — An application for anticipatory bail is strictly limited to the cause of the individual applicant apprehending arrest in a registered offence. The court's inquiry must be confined to facts relevant to the specific applicant and cannot be expanded to address third-party matters or issues beyond the scope of the original complaint [Context: The High Court exceeded its jurisdiction by issuing directions involving third parties in an anticipatory bail proceeding, which the Supreme Court set aside.] — (Paras 2, 3, 4).
  • Code of Civil Procedure, 1908 — Order 1 Rule 10 — Addition of parties — The High Court is not permitted to add third parties to anticipatory bail proceedings as if invoking civil procedural powers to implead parties who are neither necessary nor proper to the matter under consideration — (Para 4).

DHEERAJ BHADVIYA vs. STATE OF RAJASTHAN & ANR.

  • Code of Criminal Procedure, 1973 — Section 438 — Anticipatory bail — Abetment of suicide — In a case involving an application for anticipatory bail, where the accused has enjoyed interim protection for a significant period of time, the appellate court may grant relief subject to conditions such as furnishing security and cooperating with the ongoing investigation [Context: The Supreme Court allowed the appeal and directed the release of the appellant on bail in the event of arrest, subject to furnishing cash security and presenting himself before the investigating officer.] — (Para 2).
  • Indian Penal Code, 1860 — Section 306 — Abetment of suicide — Offence punishable under Section 306 of the Indian Penal Code, 1860, relates to the abetment of suicide — (Para 1).

ABHISHEK KUMAR vs. STATE OF DELHI

  • Code of Criminal Procedure, 1973 — Section 438 / Anticipatory bail — Anticipatory bail — Interim relief —Where an appellant has enjoyed interim protection from arrest for a significant period during the pendency of the appeal, the Court may grant permanent anticipatory bail upon consideration of the entirety of the facts and circumstances [Context: The Court granted anticipatory bail to the appellant who had already been protected by an interim order for over two years.] — (Paras 1, 2).

JARNAIL SINGH vs. STATE OF PUNJAB & ANR.

  • Code of Criminal Procedure, 1973 — Section 438 — Anticipatory bail — The court may grant anticipatory bail to an accused after considering the facts and circumstances of the case, provided the accused agrees to cooperate with the ongoing investigation [Context: The court granted anticipatory bail to the appellant, noting that while the investigation was complete, the appellant was required to provide sample signatures and cooperate further.] — (Para 2).

P. SURENDRAN vs. STATE BY INSPECTOR OF POLICE

  • Administrative Law — Registry function — Judicial function — The function of a court registry in numbering a petition is purely administrative. A registry cannot reject the numbering of a petition on the ground of maintainability, as such determinations fall exclusively within the domain of judicial function requiring the application of judicial mind [Context: The Supreme Court held that the High Court Registry incorrectly refused to number an anticipatory bail petition based on an office objection regarding maintainability under the SC/ST Act.] — (Paras 9, 10).
  • Scheduled Castes and the Scheduled Tribes (Prevention of Atrocities) Act, 1989 — Section 18A — Anticipatory bail — Maintainability — Determining the maintainability of an anticipatory bail petition in light of the bar under Section 18A involves judicial application of mind. Such issues must be adjudicated by an appropriate bench and cannot be preempted by administrative objections of a registry [Context: The Court directed the High Court Registry to number the petition and place it before an appropriate bench for judicial consideration.] — (Paras 6, 9, 10).

SUSHILA AGGARWAL & ORS. vs. STATE (NCT OF DELHI) & ANR.

  • Code of Criminal Procedure, 1973 — Section 438 — Anticipatory bail — Duration — Conflicting views —There exists a conflict of judicial opinion regarding whether an order of anticipatory bail under Section 438 CrPC should be for a limited duration or if it should continue until the trial. One line of decisions suggests it should be limited, while another suggests it ordinarily continues till the trial [Context: The Court noted the existence of two divergent schools of thought regarding the duration of anticipatory bail orders and determined that the issue required authoritative settlement by a larger Bench.] — (Paras 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7).

SATPAL SINGH vs. THE STATE OF PUNJAB

  • Narcotic Drugs and Psychotropic Substances Act, 1985 — Section 37 — Bail — Limitations — Commercial quantity — The limitations on grant of bail under Section 37 are in addition to the limitations prescribed under the Code of Criminal Procedure or any other law in force. When a person is accused of an offence involving a commercial quantity, he shall not be released on bail unless the court is satisfied that there are reasonable grounds for believing that he is not guilty and that he is not likely to commit any offence while on bail [Context: The High Court failed to consider the mandatory requirements of Section 37 while granting anticipatory bail for offences involving commercial quantities of narcotics.] — (Paras 3, 4, 15).
  • Code of Criminal Procedure, 1973 — Section 438 — Anticipatory bail — Regular bail — Distinction —Protection under Section 438 is available only until the court summons the accused based on the charge sheet. On appearance, the accused must seek regular bail under Section 439, which the court must decide on its own merits without assuming automatic entitlement based on the prior grant of anticipatory bail — (Para 14).

BHAUSAHEB vs. THE STATE OF MAHARASHTRA

  • Code of Criminal Procedure, 1973 — Section 438 — Anticipatory bail — Protection from arrest — In matters involving serious allegations where investigation is still in progress, the court may grant protection from arrest under Section 438 of the Code of Criminal Procedure, 1973, subject to the condition that the accused fully cooperates with the investigating agency [Context: The appellant was granted protection from arrest on the condition of executing a bond and cooperating with the ongoing investigation into a 2017 case.] — (Paras 2, 4, 5).

BIKASH MANNA vs. THE STATE OF WEST BENGAL

  • Code of Criminal Procedure, 1973 — Section 438 — Anticipatory bail — Conditions of bail — Settlement between parties — Where the de-facto complainant in a criminal case consents to the grant of anticipatory bail following a monetary settlement, the court may permit the withdrawal of deposited funds and grant protection under Section 438 of the Code of Criminal Procedure, 1973, subject to conditions including cooperation with investigation and the furnishing of bonds [Context: The Court granted anticipatory bail to the appellant after the de-facto complainant consented to the withdrawal of a deposited amount as part of a settlement.] — (Paras 3, 5, 7).

RAHUL ARORA & ORS. vs. THE STATE OF HARYANA

  • Code of Criminal Procedure, 1973 — Section 438 — Anticipatory bail — Subsequent developments — Where an accused who was previously denied anticipatory bail due to non-compliance with court-directed settlement conditions subsequently fulfills those conditions by executing the required transfers, the court may permit the accused to approach the High Court again to seek relief based on these subsequent developments [Context: The appellants were permitted to approach the High Court to seek bail after they successfully transferred property in favour of the complainant's son as previously agreed.] — (Paras 5, 6).

SATWANT SINGH vs. MALKEET SINGH

  • Contempt of Courts Act, 1971 — Section 2(b) — Civil contempt — Apology — An unconditional apology tendered by an official for actions performed in the bona fide exercise of official powers constitutes a valid defense in a civil contempt proceeding. Courts should accept such an apology where there is no evidence of a wilful or deliberate attempt to violate a court order [Context: The appellant's conviction for contempt was set aside after he tendered a sincere and unconditional apology for his actions as an investigating officer.] — (Paras 1, 4, 5, 6, 7).

RAM NARESH YADAV vs. THE STATE OF JHARKHAND & ANR.

  • Code of Criminal Procedure, 1973 — Section 438 — Anticipatory bail — Completion of investigation —Where the investigation in a criminal case has been completed and a report under Section 173(2) of the Code of Criminal Procedure has been filed, the prayer for anticipatory bail stands exhausted. The accused must appear before the concerned court upon being summoned and seek regular bail [Context: The appeal was disposed of with a direction to the appellant to appear before the court as and when summoned and seek regular bail, following the completion of investigation.] — (Paras 4, 5).

RANI DUDEJA vs. STATE OF HARYANA

  • Code of Criminal Procedure, 1973 — Section 438 — Anticipatory bail — Res judicata — The principle of res judicata does not apply to applications for anticipatory bail. A second application can be filed upon a change in circumstances, and the court is duty-bound to consider it on its merits even if a previous application was withdrawn [Context: The High Court had rejected the appellant's second bail petition on the ground that an earlier one had been withdrawn; the Supreme Court set aside this order and remanded the matter to be heard on merits.] — (Paras 3, 4).

MANISH JAIN vs. AKANKSHA JAIN

  • Hindu Marriage Act, 1955 — Section 24 — Maintenance pendente lite — Quantum of maintenance — Financial capacity — In exercising its wide discretion for granting alimony pendente lite, the court must be guided by sound principles of matrimonial law, ensuring the order is neither arbitrary nor capricious. The court must consider the income of both spouses and the status of the parties, while it is immaterial that the wife is educated or has capable parents if she lacks independent income sufficient for her support [Context: The Court reduced the maintenance amount awarded by the High Court from Rs.60,000 to Rs.25,000 per month, considering the parties' financial status.] — (Paras 11, 14, 15, 16).

RAMDEV FOOD PRODUCTS PRIVATE LIMITED vs. STATE OF GUJARAT

  • Code of Criminal Procedure, 1973 — Section 156(3) / Investigation — Magistrate's discretion — Application of mind — Pre-cognizance — The power of a Magistrate to order an investigation under Section 156(3) is a discretionary power that must be exercised with the application of mind. It is not mandatory to direct investigation for every allegation of a cognizable offence, especially in cases where the dispute is primarily of a civil nature or where there is no clear prima facie material to proceed [Context: The Court upheld the Magistrate's decision to decline a Section 156(3) investigation given the civil litigation pending between the parties.] — (Paras 11, 20, 22, 30, 34).
  • Code of Criminal Procedure, 1973 — Section 202 / Post-cognizance inquiry — Scope of inquiry — Arrest powers — Police investigation — An investigation directed by a Magistrate under Section 202 is a limited inquiry for the purpose of deciding whether there is sufficient ground to proceed. A police officer conducting such an inquiry does not possess the power to arrest the accused without warrant, as such a power would be incongruous with the preliminary stage of the proceedings [Context: The Court clarified that police officers cannot exercise arrest powers during a Section 202 inquiry.] — (Paras 15, 21, 23, 24, 25, 37).
  • Code of Criminal Procedure, 1973 — Section 154 / FIR — Mandatory registration — Preliminary inquiry —While the registration of an FIR is mandatory upon the disclosure of a cognizable offence, it is not an absolute rule that an investigation must follow in every case. Preliminary inquiries are permissible in specific categories such as commercial offences and matrimonial disputes to prevent the misuse of criminal law for settling civil scores [Context: The judgment reiterates that the law maintains a balance between the victim's right to justice and the protection of the individual's liberty.] — (Paras 17, 18, 19).

SHAKUNTLA DEVI vs. BALJINDER SINGH

  • Scheduled Castes and Scheduled Tribes (Prevention of Atrocities) Act, 1989 — Section 18 — Anticipatory bail — Statutory bar — Section 18 of the Act creates a specific bar to the grant of anticipatory bail to a person against whom an offence is registered under the provisions of the Act. No court shall entertain an application for anticipatory bail under Section 438 of the Code of Criminal Procedure, 1973 unless it, prima facie, finds that no offence under the Act is made out [Context: The High Court failed to record a finding that no offence under the Act was made out before granting anticipatory bail, rendering the order contrary to the statutory mandate.] — (Paras 4, 5).
  • Code of Criminal Procedure, 1973 — Section 438 — Anticipatory bail — Bar to relief — The power to grant anticipatory bail is excluded in cases involving an accusation of an offence under the Scheduled Castes and Scheduled Tribes (Prevention of Atrocities) Act, 1989 by virtue of Section 18 of that Act — (Paras 4, 5).

PURUSHOTTAM MEENA vs. STATE OF BIHAR & ANR.

  • Code of Criminal Procedure, 1973 — Section 438 — Anticipatory bail — Coercive action — In light of the facts and circumstances of the case, the interim order restraining coercive action against the petitioner is made absolute, subject to the conditions stipulated under Section 438 of the Code of Criminal Procedure, 1973 [Context: The Court confirmed its earlier order restraining coercive action against the appellant upon the condition that he comply with the requirements for anticipatory bail.] — (Para 4).

SAVITRI AGARWAL & ORS. vs. STATE OF MAHARASHTRA & ANR.

  • Code of Criminal Procedure, 1973 — Section 438 — Anticipatory bail — Cancellation of bail — Discretionary power — The power to grant anticipatory bail is extraordinary but not restricted to exceptional cases only. Judicial discretion must be exercised with care and circumspection based on the facts and circumstances of each case, and orders should be supported by reasons without being trapped in straight-jacket rules. Once bail is granted, it should not be cancelled unless very cogent and overwhelming circumstances are present, such as misuse of liberty or failure to cooperate with the investigation [Context: The Court held that the High Court erred in cancelling anticipatory bail where the initial order was passed after due consideration of material evidence, and no cogent circumstances like abuse of bail or non-cooperation with investigation were established.] — (Paras 10, 14, 20, 21).
  • Indian Penal Code, 1860 — Section 304-B — Dowry death — Presumption of guilt — In cases of dowry death, the statutory presumption arises against the accused when the death of a woman is caused by burns or bodily injury occurring otherwise than under normal circumstances within seven years of her marriage, provided it is shown that soon before her death, she was subjected to cruelty or harassment by her husband or his relatives for or in connection with any demand for dowry [Context: The appellants were accused of dowry death under Section 304-B.] — (Para 8).

KANCHAN KUMARI vs. THE STATE OF BIHAR & ANR.

  • Code of Criminal Procedure, 1973 — Section 438 — Anticipatory bail — Conditions of bail — Jurisdiction — A court dealing with an application for anticipatory bail must confine itself to the issues relevant to the grant or denial of such bail. It is impermissible to issue peremptory directions affecting the livelihood or rights of third parties who are not even before the court, especially without providing them notice or an opportunity to be heard [Context: The High Court had issued a direction cancelling the license of the appellant, who was not a party to the anticipatory bail proceeding, which was set aside by the Supreme Court.] — (Paras 4, 5, 6).
  • Natural Justice — Audi alteram partem — Civil consequences — Third party rights — Orders resulting in civil consequences or adversely impacting the livelihood of a party cannot be passed without issuing notice and providing an opportunity of hearing to the person so affected. Such directions issued behind the back of a non-party are legally unsustainable [Context: The Supreme Court vacated the direction cancelling the appellant's license as it was issued without notice to her, violating principles of natural justice.] — (Para 6).

Central Bureau of Investigation vs. P.S. Jayaprakash Etc. Etc.

  • Code of Criminal Procedure, 1973 — Section 438 / Anticipatory Bail — Remand — Application of mind — Consideration of individual role — In deciding an application for anticipatory bail, the Court is duty-bound to consider the gravity of the allegations, the individual role of each accused, and the specific facts and circumstances of the case. An order granting bail without addressing the individual role of the accused or the materials on record is non-speaking and liable to be set aside on remand for fresh consideration [Context: The High Court had granted anticipatory bail to the accused in a case involving espionage-related allegations without evaluating the specific roles of the accused as identified in a court-mandated investigation report.] — (Paras 4, 5, 6, 7).

Sudhir vs. The State of Maharashtra and another

  • Code of Criminal Procedure, 1973 — Section 438 / Anticipatory bail — Cancellation of anticipatory bail — Economic offences — Custodial interrogation — The discretion to grant or cancel anticipatory bail must be exercised judicially, balancing the individual's right against harassment with the needs of a free and fair investigation. In cases involving serious allegations of corruption and misappropriation of public funds, the court may cancel anticipatory bail if custodial interrogation is necessary to unearth the conspiracy and the investigation is being impeded [Context: The Court upheld the cancellation of anticipatory bail for government engineers accused of misappropriating rural development funds, noting that custodial interrogation was essential for the progress of the investigation.] — (Paras 10, 11, 12, 13).
  • Prevention of Corruption Act, 1988 — Section 13 — Corruption — Misappropriation of public funds —Allegations of corruption and misappropriation of public funds are grave matters where the grant of anticipatory bail must be weighed against the potential for harming the investigation. While no absolute bar exists, the nature of these economic offences justifies a more stringent approach to pre-arrest relief when custodial interrogation is required [Context: The Court considered the gravity of the corruption charges in affirming the cancellation of bail.] — (Paras 8, 13).

Dr. (Mrs.) Malathi Ravi, M.D. vs. Dr. B.V. Ravi, M.D.

  • Hindu Marriage Act, 1955 — Section 13(1)(ib) / Desertion — Desertion — Factum of separation — Animus deserendi — Desertion for the purpose of seeking divorce means the intentional permanent forsaking and abandonment of one spouse by the other without that other's consent and without reasonable cause. It is a total repudiation of the obligations of marriage, comprising two essential elements: the factum of separation and the intention to bring cohabitation permanently to an end. Desertion is not a single act but a continuous course of conduct to be determined by the facts and circumstances of each case [Context: The Court found that the husband failed to prove desertion for the statutory period of two years immediately preceding the petition.] — (Paras 15, 16, 17).
  • Hindu Marriage Act, 1955 — Section 13(1) / Mental cruelty — Mental cruelty — Subsequent events — Irretrievable breakdown of marriage — Mental cruelty is a state of mind and a complex human behaviour which cannot be defined by a straitjacket formula. Subsequent conduct of a spouse, even if occurring after the filing of a divorce petition, can be taken into account to determine the pattern of behaviour and establish mental cruelty. Where the relationship has deteriorated to an extent that it is beyond repair, the marriage becomes a fiction, and refusing to sever the tie does not serve the sanctity of marriage [Context: The Court granted the decree of divorce on the ground of mental cruelty, noting the wife's conduct in filing false criminal complaints and persistent uncooperative behaviour.] — (Paras 23, 25, 26, 30, 37, 38).
  • Code of Civil Procedure, 1908 — Section 151 — Inherent powers — Complete justice — Courts possess the inherent power to consider issues of mental cruelty even in the absence of a formal prayer in the relief clause, where the pleadings and evidence are on record and the litigation has spanned a lengthy period. This exercise is permissible to render complete justice and prevent parties from fighting the battle afresh after years of litigation [Context: The Court invoked this power to address mental cruelty in the absence of an amendment to the divorce petition.] — (Para 23).

TARAKANT SINGH vs. STATE OF BIHAR & ANR.

  • Criminal Procedure Code, 1973 — Section 438 / Anticipatory Bail — Anticipatory bail — Interim bail confirmation — In matters involving the rejection of anticipatory bail by the High Court, the Supreme Court may confirm an interim bail order previously passed, provided the applicant cooperates with the ongoing investigation [Context: The Court confirmed the interim bail granted earlier, effectively allowing the anticipatory bail application in the circumstances of the case.] — (Paras 3, 4).

SANCHIT ALAGH & ANR. vs. UNION OF INDIA & ORS.

  • Constitution of India, 1950 — Article 32 — Transfer of investigation — Article 32 petition — A petition under Article 32 for the transfer of investigation in multiple criminal cases to a central agency like the CBI is not maintainable where investigation in individual matters is being conducted in accordance with law and no extraordinary circumstances warranting judicial intervention are demonstrated [Context: The petitioners sought a transfer of investigation of multiple FIRs to the Central Bureau of Investigation and anticipatory bail under Article 32, but the court found no merit in such interference.] — (Paras 3, 4).
  • Code of Criminal Procedure, 1973 — Section 438 — Anticipatory bail — Alternative remedy — Where petitioners have already secured regular bail in pending matters, they must rely on those orders; otherwise, they are required to move appropriate applications for bail before the competent courts in accordance with the prescribed legal procedure [Context: The petitioners were directed to seek appropriate bail remedies in accordance with the law, noting that regular bail had already been granted in some matters.] — (Para 5).

SUNIL KUMAR RAI & ORS. vs. THE STATE OF BIHAR & ORS.

  • Constitution of India, 1950 — Article 32 — Fundamental Right — Delay — Laches — The right to approach the Supreme Court for the enforcement of Fundamental Rights under Article 32 is a Fundamental Right in itself. Delay or laches by itself without more is not sufficient to shut the doors of the court when the petition involves a violation of the fundamental right to life and personal liberty [Context: The Court overruled the preliminary objection regarding delay in challenging the 2016 notification.] — (Paras 7, 9, 11).
  • Constitution of India, 1950 — Article 342 — Scheduled Tribes — Specification — Power of President —Article 342 empowers the President, in consultation with the State, to specify the tribes or tribal communities to be treated as Scheduled Tribes. The list so specified is conclusive, and the executive government has no power to unilaterally alter or expand the list of Scheduled Tribes through administrative notifications [Context: The impugned notification attempting to include the Lohar community as Scheduled Tribes was declared illegal.] — (Paras 8, 12, 17, 22).
  • Official Languages Act, 1963 — Section 3 and Section 5 — Authoritative text — English vs Hindi — English continues to remain the authoritative text in respect of Acts of Parliament and Constitutional Orders. In case of any ambiguity or conflict between the English version and a translated Hindi version of a statute or Presidential Order, the English version shall prevail [Context: The Court rejected the contention that the Hindi version should be treated as the authoritative text.] — (Para 19).
  • Scheduled Castes and Scheduled Tribes (Prevention of Atrocities) Act, 1989 — Protection — Misuse — The protection of the 1989 Act is reserved for members of the Scheduled Castes and Scheduled Tribes. Extending such status to communities not included in the Presidential list through illegal executive notifications creates an unjustifiable inroad into the rights of the Scheduled Tribes and leads to the misuse of the Act against others [Context: The Court noted that the impugned notification was used as a handle to initiate proceedings against the petitioners.] — (Paras 5, 26, 27).
